essay:
The cost of intelligence is collapsing, and the industry has not yet reckoned with what that means. This week, MiniMax-M3 eclipsed GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on key benchmarks while running at five to ten percent of the cost [1]. Pinterest separately revealed it had gutted a frontier model's vision layer and cut its AI spend by ninety percent without meaningful quality loss [2]. NVIDIA released Cosmos 3 as an open omni-model for physical AI reasoning [3]. Three data points, one conclusion: the assumption that state-of-the-art performance requires state-of-the-art spend is breaking down faster than most enterprise AI roadmaps anticipated.

What follows from cheaper models is not simpler decisions but harder ones. If inference costs approach zero, the bottleneck becomes something else — and this week, Anthropic named it directly. Its browser agent was hijacked thirty-one percent of the time before safeguards engaged [4]. A separate analysis found that the real constraint on agentic AI adoption is not model performance but permissions — the organizational and technical gatekeeping that governs what an agent is allowed to touch [5]. Capability is now running ahead of governance, and the gap is where the real risk lives.

Enterprise software has noticed. Salesforce agreed to acquire Contentful this week [6], a move that makes more sense as AI infrastructure than as a CMS play. The logic is straightforward: as AI agents become the primary interface through which users interact with software, the underlying content and data model becomes load-bearing in ways it never was when humans were clicking through UIs. Salesforce is positioning Agentforce not as a feature but as an operating system for enterprise work, and acquisitions like this are the foundation. The company also committed two billion euros to France [7] in a bet that European enterprise AI is a separate market requiring separate investment — a thesis that the EU AI Act is steadily validating.

There is a quieter counternarrative running underneath all of this. DuckDuckGo's no-AI search engine is drawing traffic [8]. The debate over AI psychosis — whether AI systems are producing genuinely disturbing outputs or whether critics are pattern-matching noise into signal — is unresolved and gaining mainstream attention [9]. Meta is reportedly developing an AI pendant [10], another entry in the ambient AI hardware race that consumers have not yet asked for. These are not anti-AI stories so much as they are appetite stories. The market for AI is enormous but uneven, and the companies reading demand signals correctly will look different in three years from those projecting present enthusiasm forward indefinitely.

The most significant event of the week is probably Anthropic filing confidentially to go public [11]. This is not primarily a financing story. It is a legitimacy story. Frontier AI labs have operated with unusual opacity — substantial funding, enormous influence, limited accountability to public markets. An Anthropic IPO changes the information environment around AI safety and capabilities in ways that are hard to predict but likely to matter. The questions that public market analysts ask are different from the questions that private investors ask, and those questions tend to find their way into public discourse. The companies that shaped the current AI moment will now be asked to explain themselves to a broader audience. That is probably good.

citations.title: MiniMax-M3 debuts, eclipsing GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on key benchmarks for just 5-10% of the cost

Salesforce Signs Definitive Agreement to Acquire Contentful
Salesforce Deepens Commitment to France with $2 Billion Investment
DuckDuckGo makes its 'no-AI' search engine easier to access as its traffic booms
